results.question: What urinary biomarker panel did this paper validate?
results.note: The provided evidence discusses a CT-based pancreatic cancer classifier and does not mention any urinary biomarker panel. No urinary biomarker validation is described in [Source 1]-[Source 4].

results.question: What MRI segmentation model was trained in this study?
results.note: The evidence describes a CNN classifier for pancreatic cancer on CT images, not an MRI segmentation model [Source 2][Source 4].

results.question: What legal framework for AI regulation was proposed by the authors?
results.note: The provided evidence is about a deep learning model for pancreatic cancer detection and contains no discussion of AI regulation or any proposed legal framework [Source 1][Source 2][Source 4].

results.question: What chemotherapy regimen was recommended for treatment?
results.note: The provided evidence does not mention any specific chemotherapy regimen being recommended. It only generally states that chemotherapy can offer benefit in localized pancreatic cancer [Source 1], but no regimen is named.

results.question: What was the whole-slide histopathology magnification used in the experiments?
results.note: The provided evidence is about a CT-based pancreatic cancer classifier and does not mention whole-slide histopathology or any histopathology magnification [Source 3][Source 4].

AIM
To identify pancreatic cancer in computed tomography (CT) images automatically
by constructing a convolutional neural network (CNN) classifier.
METHODS
A CNN model was constructed using a dataset of 3494 CT images obtained from
222 patients with pathologically confirmed pancreatic cancer and 3751 CT images
from 190 patients with normal pancreas from June 2017 to June 2018. We
establishe

establishe

plain scan, arterial phase, and venous phase, respectively. The sensitivity of the CNN
(known as recall in cancer detection - the correctly predicted malignant lesions divided
by all the malignant lesions) was 91.58%, 94.08%, and 92.28% on the plain scan, arterial
phase, and venous phase images, respectively. The

AIM

results.question: What was the goal of the pancreas7 study?
snippets: cancer, and 190 diagnosed with normal pancreas were included as a control group.

Research objectives
To identify pancreatic cancer in computed tomography (CT) images automatically by
constructing a convolutional neural network (CNN) classifier.

The primary goal for a CNN classifier is to detect pancreatic cancer effectively, thus,
the model needs to consider sensitivity as a priority over specificity.

Second, our dataset was created using a
database with pancreatic cancer/normal pancreas ratio of approximately 1:1; thus, the
risk of malignancy in our study cohort was much higher than the normal real-world
rate, which made model calculations easier.

Research methods
A CNN model was constructed using a dataset of 3494 CT images obtained from 222
patients with pathologically confirmed pancreatic cancer and 3751 CT images from 190
patients with normal pancreas from June 2017 to June 2018.

results.question: What CNN architecture did the paper use?
snippets:
The architecture of our proposed CNN model is
presented in Figure 2.

Among the available deep learning architecture, the CNN is
the most commonly applied algorithm for analyzing visual images; it can receive an
input image, assign weights to various aspects of the image and distinguish one type
of image content from another[17].

A convolutional neural network (CNN) is a
class of neural network models that can extract features from images by exploring the
local spatial correlations presented in images.
Figure 2 Architecture of our convolutional neural network model.

The CNN acts like a
black box, and it can make judgments independent of prior experience or the human
effort involved in creating manual features, which is a major advantage.

results.question: What CT phases were included in the datasets?
snippets:
Training and testing the CNN: We collected three types of CT images: Plain scan,
venous phase, and arterial phase and built three datasets from the collected images
based on the image types.

Table 1 Statistics of our datasets
Number of patients                                            Number of images
CT phase                                           With cancer                                                     With cancer
                                Without cancer cancer                                        Total   Without cancer                                      Total

For each CT scan, one to nine pictures of the pancreas were selected
from each phase.

Performance of the deep convolutional neural network used as a binary classifier
Datasets of 3494 CT images obtained from 222 patients with pathologically confirmed
pancreatic cancer and 3751 CT images from 190 patients with normal pancreas were
included, statistics of each dataset are presented in Table 1.

Finally, datasets of 3494 CT images obtained from 222 patients with
pathologically confirmed pancreatic cancer and 3751 CT images from 190 patients with
normal pancreas were collected.

METHODS
A CNN model was constructed using a dataset of 3494 CT images obtained from
222 patients with pathologically confirmed pancreatic cancer and 3751 CT images
from 190 patients with normal pancreas from June 2017 to June 2018.

Research methods
A CNN model was constructed using a dataset of 3494 CT images obtained from 222
patients with pathologically confirmed pancreatic cancer and 3751 CT images from 190
patients with normal pancreas from June 2017 to June 2018.

A total of 343
patients were pathologically diagnosed with pancreatic cancer from June 2017 to June
2018.

Datasets of 3494 CT images obtained from 222 patients with pathologically confirmed
pancreatic cancer and 3751 CT images from 190 patients with normal pancreas were
included, statistics of each dataset are presented in Table 1.

Thus, among the 412 enrolled subjects, 222 were
pathologically diagnosed with pancreatic cancer, and the remaining 190 diagnosed
with normal pancreas were included as a control group.

Finally, datasets of 3494 CT images obtained from 222 patients with
pathologically confirmed pancreatic cancer and 3751 CT images from 190 patients with
normal pancreas were collected.

A total of 343
patients were pathologically diagnosed with pancreatic cancer from June 2017 to June
2018.

Data collection and preparation
Dataset: Between June 2017 and June 2018, patients with pathologically diagnosed
pancreatic cancer in the First Affiliated Hospital, Zhejiang University School of
Medicine, China, were eligible for inclusion in the present study.

results.question: What binary classification accuracy did the CNN achieve across the three CT phases?
snippets:
The overall diagnostic accuracy of the ternary classifier
CNN was 82.06%, 79.06%, and 78.80% on the plain scan, arterial phase, and venous
phase, respectively.

In the trained ternary classifier, the overall
diagnostic accuracy of the ternary classifier CNN was 82.06%, 79.06%, and 78.80%
on plain phase, arterial phase, and venous phase, respectively.

Then, we constructed a binary classifier using our
CNN model by 10-fold cross validation on 2094, 2592, and 2559 images in the plain
scan, arterial phase, and venous phase, respectively (Table 1).

In the constructed
binary classifier, all three phases had high levels of accuracy and sensitivity, with no
significant differences among the three phases.

We built three datasets
from our images according to the image phases, evaluated our approach in terms of
binary classification and ternary classification using 10-fold cross validation, and
measured the effectiveness of the model with regard to the accuracy, sensitivity, and
specificity.

results.question: How did the CNN compare with gastroenterologists and trainees on plain scan accuracy?
snippets:
The CNN
and board-certified gastroenterologists achieved higher accuracies than trainees
on plain scan diagnosis (χ2 = 21.534, P < 0.001; χ2 = 9.524, P < 0.05; respectively).

When compared
with the judgments of gastroenterologists and trainees on the plain phase, the CNN
model achieved good performance.

The accuracy of the CNN and board-certified
gastroenterologists was higher than that of the trainees; however, the difference
between CNN and gastroenterologists was not significant.

The accuracy
of the gastroenterologists, trainees, and the CNN was 92.20%, 73.60%, and 95.47%,

results.question: Which phase had the highest sensitivity for head and neck pancreatic malignancies in the ternary classifier?
snippets:
In the ternary classifier, the arterial
phase had the highest sensitivity in detecting cancer in the head of the pancreas among
three phases, but it achieved only moderate performances.

In the ternary classifier, the arterial phase had
the highest sensitivity in detecting cancer in the head of the pancreas among three
phases, but it achieved only moderate performances.

In the ternary classifier, the arterial
phase had the highest sensitivity in detecting cancer in the head of the pancreas among the
three phases.

Regarding sensitivity, the arterial phase had the highest sensitivity in
finding malignant lesions among all malignancies in the pancreas head.

Difference in sensitivity for cancers in the head among the three
phases was significant (χ2 = 16.651, P < 0.001), with arterial phase having the
highest sensitivity.

results.question: Why did the authors say plain phase could be sufficient for the binary classifier?
snippets:
Considering that plain phase had relatively same sensitivity, easier
access, and lower radiation compared with arterial phase and venous phase, it is more
sufficient for the binary classifier.

Considering that the plain phase had same
sensitivity, easier access, and lower radiation compared with arterial phase and
venous phase , it is more sufficient for the binary classifier.

Thus, these results indicated that the plain scan alone
might be sufficient for the binary classifier.

Our results indicate that
the plain scan, which has easier access and lower radiation, is sufficient for the binary
classifier, with an AUC 0.9653.

In the binary classifier, the performance of
plain, arterial and venous phase had no difference.

results.question: What was the goal of the pancreas7 study?
results.answer_preview: To identify pancreatic cancer in CT images automatically using a CNN classifier [Source 1].

results.question: What CNN architecture did the paper use?
What CT phases were included in the datasets?
How many patients and images were included in total?
How many pancreatic cancer patients and control patients were used in the binary classification experiments?
results.answer_preview: 222 pancreatic cancer patients and 190 control patients

results.question: What binary classification accuracy did the CNN achieve across the three CT phases?
How did the CNN compare with gastroenterologists and trainees on plain scan accuracy?
results.answer_preview: The CNN and board-certified gastroenterologists both outperformed trainees on plain scan diagnosis, and the difference between the CNN and gastroenterologists was not significant [Source 2].

results.question: Which phase had the highest sensitivity for head and neck pancreatic malignancies in the ternary classifier?
Why did the authors say plain phase could be sufficient for the binary classifier?
What was the goal of the pancreas7 study?
results.answer_preview: To automatically identify pancreatic cancer in CT images by building a CNN classifier [Source 2].

results.question: What CNN architecture did the paper use?
What CT phases were included in the datasets?
results.answer_preview: Plain scan, venous phase, and arterial phase [Source 1].

results.question: How many patients and images were included in total?
How many pancreatic cancer patients and control patients were used in the binary classification experiments?
results.answer_preview: 222 pancreatic cancer patients and 190 control patients.

results.question: What binary classification accuracy did the CNN achieve across the three CT phases?
How did the CNN compare with gastroenterologists and trainees on plain scan accuracy?
results.answer_preview: The CNN had higher plain scan accuracy than trainees and was not significantly different from board-certified gastroenterologists [Source 1][Source 4][Source 5].

results.question: Which phase had the highest sensitivity for head and neck pancreatic malignancies in the ternary classifier?
Why did the authors say plain phase could be sufficient for the binary classifier?
results.answer_preview: Because plain phase had similar sensitivity/performance to arterial and venous phases, while being easier to access and involving lower radiation [Source 1][Source 5].
